title: Changelog
text:
ferx_fit() now returns $gradient_used — the inner-loop gradient method the engine actually used ("ad", "fd", or "N/A"). When gradient = "auto" it shows which branch resolved at fit time; ODE models silently fall back from AD to FD even when gradient = "ad" was requested, and this field surfaces that fallback without needing to read warnings. The raw engine labels are also exposed as $gradient_method_inner / $gradient_method_outer. print() shows both requested and used; summary() formats them as auto (requested) -> ad (used) (ferx-core#1).



ferx_model() argument order is now ferx_model(data, model) (data first). This enables the natural data-first pipe entry point ex$data |> ferx_model(ex$model) |> ferx_fit() (#81).
Old-style positional calls (ferx_model("pk.ferx") or ferx_model("pk.ferx", "data.csv")) are detected by the .ferx extension on what is now the data argument and auto-corrected with a deprecation warning. The compatibility shim will be removed in a future release. Calls that pass data by name (ferx_model("pk.ferx", data = "data.csv")) keep working unchanged because R matches data = by name first and the remaining positional argument falls into the model slot.



ferx_set_section() now applies copy-on-write when the underlying ferx_model points at a file inside the installed ferx package directory (e.g. a model returned by ferx_example()). The file is copied to tempdir() before editing and the returned ferx_model’s $model field is updated to the copy, preventing accidental modification of bundled examples. Plain-path callers are unaffected — passing a path string still edits in place (#80).
ferx_check_init() now accepts a ferx_model as its first argument (in addition to a plain path), so it can be placed directly in a pipe: ex$data |> ferx_model(ex$model) |> ferx_check_init(). When a ferx_model is supplied and data is not, the data path on the object is used (#79).


New vignette “Editing ferx model files programmatically” covering ferx_model_new(), ferx_model_section(), and ferx_model_set_section(): skeleton creation, section inspection, read-modify-write patterns, a console-only fit workflow, and overwrite-guard behaviour.

title: Changelog
text: ferx_fit() now returns $gradient_used — the inner-loop gradient method the engine actually used ("ad", "fd", or "N/A"). When gradient = "auto" it shows which branch resolved at fit time; ODE models silently fall back from AD to FD even when gradient = "ad" was requested, and this field surfaces that fallback without needing to read warnings. The raw engine labels are also exposed as $gradient_method_inner / $gradient_method_outer. print() shows both requested and used; summary() formats them as auto (requested) -> ad (used) (ferx-core#1).

title: Changelog
text:
ferx_model() argument order is now ferx_model(data, model) (data first). This enables the natural data-first pipe entry point ex$data |> ferx_model(ex$model) |> ferx_fit() (#81).
Old-style positional calls (ferx_model("pk.ferx") or ferx_model("pk.ferx", "data.csv")) are detected by the .ferx extension on what is now the data argument and auto-corrected with a deprecation warning. The compatibility shim will be removed in a future release. Calls that pass data by name (ferx_model("pk.ferx", data = "data.csv")) keep working unchanged because R matches data = by name first and the remaining positional argument falls into the model slot.

title: Changelog
text:
ferx_set_section() now applies copy-on-write when the underlying ferx_model points at a file inside the installed ferx package directory (e.g. a model returned by ferx_example()). The file is copied to tempdir() before editing and the returned ferx_model’s $model field is updated to the copy, preventing accidental modification of bundled examples. Plain-path callers are unaffected — passing a path string still edits in place (#80).
ferx_check_init() now accepts a ferx_model as its first argument (in addition to a plain path), so it can be placed directly in a pipe: ex$data |> ferx_model(ex$model) |> ferx_check_init(). When a ferx_model is supplied and data is not, the data path on the object is used (#79).

title: Changelog
text: New vignette “Editing ferx model files programmatically” covering ferx_model_new(), ferx_model_section(), and ferx_model_set_section(): skeleton creation, section inspection, read-modify-write patterns, a console-only fit workflow, and overwrite-guard behaviour.

title: Changelog
text:
New features

New ferx_model_validate(path) checks a .ferx file for syntax errors and missing required sections without running the optimizer. Prints a section presence report and returns TRUE/FALSE invisibly. Required sections are [parameters], [individual_parameters], [structural_model], [error_model], and [initial_values]; [odes] and [fit_options] are optional.
ferx_fit() now returns $eigenvalues (sorted descending) and $condition_number (ratio of largest to smallest eigenvalue) for the covariance correlation matrix. Both are NULL when the covariance step was not run or failed. condition_number = Inf signals a non-positive eigenvalue. A warning is appended when condition_number > 1000. The condition number is shown on the Covariance: line in print() and summary() output.
ferx_model_inspect(path) parses a .ferx file without fitting and prints a compact structural summary (model type, IIV, IOV, residual error). Pass a ferx_fit object instead of a path to inspect the structure post-fit without re-supplying the file.
ferx_fit() now attaches $model_structure to every result: a named list with fields theta_names, model_type, iiv, iov, and residual. The same summary is shown in print() and summary() output.
ferx_model_section(path, section) — extract and print the body of a named section from a .ferx model file; returns lines invisibly for scripted use.
ferx_model_set_section(path, section, lines) — replace the body of a named section in-place; the write complement to ferx_model_section().

title: Changelog
text:
Documentation

New vignette “Model workflow: inspect, edit, fit” (vignette("model-workflow", package = "ferx")) demonstrates the pre-fit inspection workflow: ferx_model_inspect() before ferx_fit() to catch structural mistakes cheaply, and re-inspecting the fitted result via ferx_model_inspect(fit) (closes #57).

title: Changelog
text:
Changes to existing functions

ferx_fit() now returns $sigma_names and $sigma_types (parallel to $sigma), and print() displays each sigma with its declared name, the derived variance (sigma^2), and — for proportional components — the CV% (sigma * 100). Sigma is on the standard-deviation scale for both proportional and additive components, matching the new YAML output added in ferx-core#57. Closes #59.
result$model_structure is now sourced from the Rust engine (built from the parsed CompiledModel) instead of an R-side regex re-parse of the .ferx file (closes ferx-core#49). The shape is unchanged — theta_names, model_type, iiv, iov, residual — so ferx_model_inspect() callers see the same fields. model_type now distinguishes IV bolus from infusion (e.g. "1-cpt IV infusion") and adds 3-cpt variants; the pre-fit ferx_model_inspect(path) parser was updated to the same label set so both the file-based and fit-based inspection paths report identical strings.
ferx_model_edit() gains overwrite = FALSE. Previously the function silently skipped the file copy when the destination already existed; it now errors with a clear message. Callers that relied on the silent-skip must add overwrite = TRUE.
ferx_model_new() gains print = FALSE. When print = TRUE the skeleton is printed to the console without writing any file or opening an editor; path becomes optional in that mode. Five templates are available: "1cpt_oral" (default), "1cpt_iv", "2cpt_oral", "2cpt_iv", "ode".

title: Changelog
text:
Bug fixes

print.ferx_fit() now uses the exact coefficient of variation formula for EXP(OMEGA) log-normal parameters: sqrt(exp(omega) - 1) * 100 instead of the approximation sqrt(omega) * 100 (doi:10.1002/psp4.12507). Applied only when eta_param_types == "log_normal" (defaults to log-normal when the field is absent). Display for logit, additive, and custom ETA types is deferred to #53.
ferx_model_section(): fixed a descending-index bug where an empty section body (header immediately followed by another header) returned lines in reverse instead of character(0).
ferx_model_set_section(): fixed a last-section splice bug where seq.int(end+1, length) produced a descending sequence and appended NA plus a duplicate line when replacing the last section in a file.
ferx_estimates(): estimate_natural is now NA when SE is unavailable, matching the documented contract that all natural-scale columns are NA when the covariance step was not run. Previously the back-transform was always populated for log and logit thetas regardless of SE.
.ferx_model_type() (used by ferx_model_inspect()): now returns "X-cpt IV infusion" for *_infusion PK models, matching the label the Rust engine attaches post-fit. Previously the pre-fit label dropped the IV token.
print.ferx_fit(): the logit-ETA +/-1SD summary line is now ASCII; the previous ± rendered as <U+00B1> under non-UTF-8 locales.

title: Output Reference
text: A ferx-core run produces diagnostic data and parameter estimates that are exposed to R through the FitResult struct. The R layer formats these as data frames, named lists, and S3 objects. The CLI writes them as CSV and YAML files.

title: Output Reference
text:
sdtab — per-observation diagnostics
FitResult::sdtab is a Vec<SdtabRow> exposed to R as a data frame via sdtab_to_dataframe(). The CLI writes it as {model}-sdtab.csv.

Columns



Column
Description




ID
Subject identifier


TIME
Observation time


DV
Observed value


PRED
Population prediction (ETA = 0)


IPRED
Individual prediction (ETA = EBE)


CWRES
Conditional weighted residual


IWRES
Individual weighted residual


ETA1, ETA2, …
Empirical Bayes estimates of each random effect


EBE_OFV
Subject’s contribution to the total OFV


N_OBS
Number of observations for the subject





Residual definitions
IWRES (Individual Weighted Residual):
\[\text{IWRES}_j = \frac{y_j - \text{IPRED}_j}{\sqrt{V_j}}\]
where \(V_j\) is the residual variance evaluated at the individual prediction (\(\hat{\eta}\)). Measures how well the individual model fits each observation.
CWRES (Conditional Weighted Residual):
\[\text{CWRES}_j = \frac{y_j - f_{0,j}}{\sqrt{\tilde{R}_{jj}}}\]
where \(f_0 = f(\hat{\eta}) - H\hat{\eta}\) is the linearised population prediction and \(\tilde{R} = H\Omega H^T + R\) is the conditional variance matrix. CWRES accounts for the linearisation; it is the residual most appropriate for assessing population model fit.

title: Output Reference
text:
Shrinkage
Reported in FitResult after every fit. High shrinkage (near 1) indicates that the data are not informative about a random effect — individual ETAs collapse toward zero.
ETA shrinkage (per random effect, shrinkage_eta: Vec<f64>):
\[\text{shrinkage}_k = 1 - \frac{\text{SD}(\hat{\eta}_k)}{\sqrt{\omega_{kk}}}\]
EPS shrinkage (scalar, shrinkage_eps: f64):
\[\text{shrinkage}_\varepsilon = 1 - \text{SD}(\text{IWRES})\]
Values of NaN indicate a zero-variance omega component (ETA) or fewer than two valid residuals (EPS).

title: Output Reference
text:
Fit YAML ({model}-fit.yaml)
The CLI writes a YAML summary alongside the sdtab. Structure:
model:
  converged: true
  method: focei
objective_function:
  ofv: -280.1838
  aic: -266.1838
  bic: -247.2804
data:
  n_subjects: 10
  n_observations: 110
  n_parameters: 7
theta:
  TVCL:
    estimate: 0.132735
    se: 0.014549
    rse_pct: 11.0
  TVV:
    estimate: 7.694842
    se: 0.293028
    rse_pct: 3.8
omega:
  omega_11:
    variance: 0.028584
    cv_pct: 16.9
    se: 0.006394
  omega_22:
    variance: 0.009613
    cv_pct: 9.8
    se: 0.002165
sigma:
  sigma_1:
    estimate: 0.010638
    se: 0.000788
Key fields:

ofv — −2 log-likelihood
aic / bic — OFV + 2p / OFV + p·ln(N); p = estimated parameters, N = observations
rse_pct — relative SE as percentage: |SE / estimate| × 100
cv_pct (omega) — coefficient of variation: \(100\sqrt{\omega_{kk}}\)
se — standard error from the finite-difference covariance step

title: Output Reference
text:
Covariance status
FitResult::covariance_status is one of:







Value
Meaning




Computed
Covariance step succeeded; SE values are valid


Failed
Hessian was singular or inversion failed; SE fields are None


NotRequested
covariance = false; SE fields are None

title: Output Reference
text:
Run record fields







Field
Description




model_name
Name from the .ferx file (or "Unnamed")


ferx_version
Version of ferx-nlme that produced the result


wall_time_secs
Wall-clock time for the complete fit (seconds)


gradient_used
Gradient method resolved at runtime: "enzyme", "dual", or "finite_differences"


uses_ode_solver
true for ODE models, false for analytical PK


n_threads_used
Number of rayon threads during estimation

title: Output Reference
text:
EBE convergence diagnostics
Counters from the inner-loop (EBE) optimizer; always 0 for SAEM.







Field
Description




ebe_convergence_warnings
Outer iterations in which at least one subject had an unconverged EBE


max_unconverged_subjects
Worst-case unconverged count in a single outer iteration


total_ebe_fallbacks
Times the Nelder-Mead fallback was invoked across all subjects and iterations



If ebe_convergence_warnings is large relative to maxiter, the inner-loop tolerance (inner_tol) may be too tight, or a subject’s data are poorly compatible with the model.

title: Output Reference
text:
Optimizer trace CSV
When optimizer_trace = true in [fit_options], ferx-core writes a per-iteration CSV to /tmp/ferx_trace_<pid>_<ts>.csv; the path is stored in FitResult::trace_path.
Each row is one outer iteration. Unused columns contain NA.








Column
Populated by
Description




iter
all
Iteration number


method
all
foce, focei, gn, gn_hybrid, saem


phase
gn_hybrid, saem
explore / converge / focei (GN polish)


ofv
all
Objective function value


wall_ms
all
Wall time for this iteration (ms)


grad_norm
BFGS, NLopt gradient-mode
Gradient norm


step_norm
BFGS
Step size


optimizer
FOCE/FOCEI
Active NLopt algorithm


lm_lambda
GN
Levenberg-Marquardt damping factor


ofv_delta
GN
Change in OFV from previous iteration


step_accepted
GN
Whether the GN step was accepted


cond_nll
SAEM
Conditional observation NLL


gamma
SAEM
SAEM step-size (1 in exploration, 1/k in convergence)


mh_accept_rate
SAEM
Mean MH acceptance rate across subjects


n_ebe_unconverged
FOCE/FOCEI
Subjects whose inner optimizer did not converge


n_ebe_fallback
FOCE/FOCEI
Subjects that fell back to Nelder-Mead



The trace is buffered and flushed at fit end; the tail may be lost if the process is killed mid-run.
In R:
fit <- ferx_fit("model.ferx", "data.csv", optimizer_trace = TRUE)
trace <- read.csv(fit$trace_path)
plot(trace$iter, trace$ofv, type = "l", xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "OFV")

title: Output Reference
text:
Relevant source files







File
Role




io/output.rs
sdtab CSV writer, fit YAML writer, timing file


types.rs
FitResult, SdtabRow, CovarianceStatus, shrinkage fields


stats/likelihood.rs
CWRES / PRED computation (linearisation)


stats/residual_error.rs
IWRES and residual variance per error model


estimation/trace.rs
Optimizer trace CSV buffered writer

title: Overview
text:
ferx-core is the Rust computation engine that powers the ferx R package. It implements the full nonlinear mixed effects (NLME) modeling pipeline: model parsing, data ingestion, population parameter estimation (FOCE/FOCEI, SAEM, Gauss-Newton), simulation, and prediction. The R package (ferx-r) is a thin wrapper that exposes this engine via an extendr FFI boundary.
The crate is named ferx-nlme and lives at github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-core. ferx-r references it as a path dependency in src/rust/Cargo.toml.

title: Overview
text:
Stack position
R API  (ferx-r:  R/*.R)
  └─► extendr FFI  (ferx-r:  src/rust/src/lib.rs)
        └─► ferx-core  (estimation, PK, ODE, autodiff)
The FFI layer exposes three #[extendr] functions — ferx_rust_fit, ferx_rust_simulate, ferx_rust_predict — and converts ferx-core result types into R lists and data frames via fit_result_to_list() and sdtab_to_dataframe(). All heavy computation stays inside ferx-core.

title: Overview
text:
Crate modules








Module
File(s)
Responsibility




api
api.rs
Public entry points: run_model_with_data, simulate, predict


types
types.rs
Shared types: Subject, DoseEvent, PkParams, ModelParameters, Population, FitResult


parser
parser/model_parser.rs
Parses .ferx model files into a CompiledModel


io
io/datareader.rs, io/output.rs
NONMEM CSV reader, sdtab writer


estimation
estimation/outer_optimizer.rs, inner_optimizer.rs, saem.rs, trust_region.rs, gauss_newton.rs, parameterization.rs, sir.rs, trace.rs
FOCE/FOCEI, SAEM, Gauss-Newton, SIR, iteration trace


pk
pk/one_compartment.rs, two_compartment.rs, three_compartment.rs, event_driven.rs
Analytical 1/2/3-cpt PK models and event dispatch


ode
ode/solver.rs, ode/predictions.rs
RK45 (Dormand-Prince) adaptive ODE solver


stats
stats/likelihood.rs, residual_error.rs, special.rs
FOCE/FOCEI NLL, CWRES/IWRES, special functions


ad
ad/dual.rs, ad/ad_gradients.rs, ad/event_driven_ad.rs
Forward-mode Dual type and Enzyme reverse-mode AD wrappers

title: Overview
text:
Key return type: FitResult
FitResult is the struct returned through the FFI to R. Its principal fields:








Field
Type
Description




converged
bool
Whether the outer optimizer converged


method
String
Estimation method used (e.g. "focei")


gradient_used
String
AD mode resolved at runtime ("enzyme", "dual", "finite_differences")


ofv
f64
Objective function value (−2 log-likelihood)


aic
f64
Akaike information criterion


bic
f64
Bayesian information criterion


theta
Vec<f64>
Final population fixed-effect estimates


theta_names
Vec<String>
Names matching theta


se_theta
Vec<f64>
Standard errors for theta (requires covariance = true)


omega
DMatrix<f64>
Final between-subject variability matrix


se_omega
Vec<f64>
Standard errors for omega elements


sigma
Vec<f64>
Residual error estimates


sdtab
Vec<SdtabRow>
Per-observation diagnostics: PRED, IPRED, CWRES, IWRES, ETAs


warnings
Vec<String>
Non-fatal warnings emitted during the run

title: Overview
text:
Further reading

Architecture — data flow, FFI boundary, key types, parallelism, source tree
Estimation — FOCE/FOCEI, SAEM, Gauss-Newton, IOV, SIR
Analytical PK models — 1/2/3-compartment closed-form solutions
ODE solver — RK45 adaptive integrator and event handling
Autodiff — Dual type and Enzyme reverse-mode integration

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text: ferx-core supports five estimation methods: FOCE, FOCEI, SAEM, Gauss-Newton, and a GN+FOCEI hybrid. FOCEI is the default. All methods share the same two-loop structure: an outer loop over population parameters and an inner loop over per-subject empirical Bayes estimates (EBEs).

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
FOCE vs FOCEI
FOCE (First-Order Conditional Estimation) linearises the model around the EBE η̂ and evaluates the residual variance R at the population mean (η = 0):
\[\text{OFV}_i = (y - f_0)^T \tilde{R}^{-1} (y - f_0) + \log|\tilde{R}|\]
where \(f_0 = f(\hat{\eta}) - H\hat{\eta}\) and \(\tilde{R} = H\Omega H^T + R(f_0)\).
FOCEI adds the interaction term — the residual variance \(V(\hat{\eta})\) is evaluated at η̂ rather than η = 0, capturing the dependence of residual error on individual predictions:
\[\text{OFV}_i = (y - \hat{f})^T V^{-1} (y - \hat{f}) + \hat{\eta}^T \Omega^{-1} \hat{\eta} + \log|\tilde{R}|\]
FOCEI is more accurate when the error model is proportional or combined. Use FOCE only for additive-error models where the interaction term is zero.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
The two-loop structure

Outer loop — population parameters
Minimises the population OFV = Σᵢ OFVᵢ over θ, Ω, σ. Parameters are internally log-transformed (theta, sigma) and Cholesky-factored (omega) so the optimizer works on an unconstrained packed vector:
x = [log(θ_free), chol(Ω_BSV), log(σ), chol(Ω_IOV)]
Available optimizers (set via optimizer in [fit_options]):








Key
Algorithm
Notes




slsqp
NLopt SLSQP
Default. Gradient-based, handles bounds well


bobyqa
NLopt BOBYQA
Derivative-free; robust when FD gradients are noisy


nlopt_lbfgs
NLopt L-BFGS
Good for large parameter spaces


mma
NLopt MMA
Method of Moving Asymptotes


bfgs
Built-in BFGS
Quasi-Newton with backtracking line search


lbfgs
Built-in L-BFGS
Memory-efficient BFGS


trust_region
Newton trust-region (argmin)
Uses FD Hessian; Steihaug CG subproblem



Global search: setting global_search = true runs NLopt CRS2-LM (Controlled Random Search) as a gradient-free pre-search before the local optimizer. Helps escape local minima on poorly-identified models. Budget is controlled by global_maxeval (default: 200 * (n_params + 1)).


Inner loop — empirical Bayes estimates
Minimises the individual NLL per subject independently (parallelised via rayon):
\[\text{NLL}(\eta) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\eta^T\Omega^{-1}\eta + \log|\Omega| + \sum_j\left(\frac{(y_j - f_j(\hat{\eta}))^2}{V_j(\hat{\eta})} + \log V_j(\hat{\eta})\right)\right]\]
Uses L-BFGS with automatic differentiation gradients (preferred) or finite differences. Falls back to Nelder-Mead simplex if L-BFGS fails to converge.
Warm starting: EBEs from the previous outer iteration seed the inner optimizer, drastically reducing inner iterations in later stages when parameters change slowly.
EBE convergence guard: if more than max_unconverged_frac (default 0.1) of subjects have unconverged EBEs, the outer optimizer receives OFV = ∞ for that step, preventing convergence to a solution with unreliable EBEs.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Convergence criteria



Loop
Criterion
Default




Outer
gradient norm < outer_gtol
1e-6


Outer
max iterations
maxiter = 500


Inner
gradient norm < inner_tol
1e-4


Inner
max iterations
inner_maxiter = 200



inner_tol = 1e-4 matches the precision of NONMEM’s default inner-loop convergence (SIGDIGITS ≈ 3). Tighter values over-converge EBEs relative to the Sheiner-Beal linearisation error and can slow FOCEI fits 10–15× without changing the final OFV.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
OFV / AIC / BIC
\[\text{OFV} = -2\log L \qquad \text{AIC} = \text{OFV} + 2p \qquad \text{BIC} = \text{OFV} + p\log N\]
where \(p\) is the number of estimated parameters and \(N\) is the number of observations.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Covariance step
When covariance = true, ferx computes the observed Fisher information matrix via finite-difference Hessian of the population OFV at convergence:

Standard errors = \(\sqrt{\text{diag}(\hat{\Sigma})}\)
%RSE = \(|\text{SE} / \hat{\theta}| \times 100\)
Omega SEs derived via delta method from the Cholesky parameterisation

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
SAEM
Stochastic Approximation EM replaces the deterministic inner loop with Metropolis-Hastings sampling, following the Monolix two-phase schedule:

Phase 1 (exploration, γ = 1): rapid basin finding; default 150 iterations
Phase 2 (convergence, γ = 1/k): decreasing-weight average; default 250 iterations

Each SAEM iteration:

E-step: sample η per subject via MH random walk (\(\eta_\text{prop} = \eta + \delta_i \cdot L \cdot z\), \(z \sim N(0,I)\))
SA update: \(S_2 \leftarrow (1-\gamma_k)S_2 + \gamma_k \cdot \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \eta_i\eta_i^T\)
M-step for Ω: \(\Omega_k = S_2\) (closed form, with structural zeros projected out)
M-step for θ and σ: NLopt minimises the conditional observation NLL with ETAs fixed. When mu_referencing = true (default), log-normal thetas use the closed-form EM update instead: \(\log\theta_j \leftarrow \log\theta_j + \gamma_k\overline{\eta_j}\)
Adaptation: per-subject MH step sizes \(\delta_i\) adjusted every adapt_interval iterations to target ~40% acceptance rate

Post-SAEM: standard FOCE inner loop refines EBEs from the SAEM ETAs, then FOCE OFV, AIC, BIC, and (optionally) the covariance matrix are computed so results are directly comparable with FOCE fits.
[fit_options]
  method        = saem
  n_exploration = 150
  n_convergence = 250
  n_mh_steps    = 3
  seed          = 12345
  covariance    = true
SAEM + FOCEI polish (recommended for complex models):
[fit_options]
  method     = [saem, focei]
  covariance = true
Limitation: SAEM is not supported with IOV models.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Gauss-Newton
The Gauss-Newton optimizer exploits the nonlinear-least-squares structure of the FOCE objective using the BHHH Hessian approximation:
\[H_\text{BHHH} = 4 \sum_i g_i g_i^T\]
where \(g_i = \nabla_x \text{NLL}_i\) is the per-subject gradient. Levenberg-Marquardt damping \(H_\text{LM} = H_\text{BHHH} + \lambda\,\text{diag}(H_\text{BHHH})\) stabilises steps away from the minimum; \(\lambda\) adapts: ×0.3 on success, ×10 on rejection.








Method
Key
Description




Pure GN
gn
Typically converges in 10–30 iterations


GN + FOCEI hybrid
gn_hybrid
GN finds the basin; FOCEI polishes (up to 100 iter via SLSQP)



Use gn for fast feedback during model development; gn_hybrid when you want robustness as well as speed.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Inter-Occasion Variability (IOV)
IOV models occasion-to-occasion shifts in PK parameters within a subject. Kappa (κ) is the IOV random effect, re-drawn independently per occasion:
\[\kappa_{ik} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \Omega_\text{IOV})\]
Option A — diagonal IOV (independent kappas):
[parameters]
  kappa KAPPA_CL ~ 0.05
  kappa KAPPA_V  ~ 0.03

[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL + KAPPA_CL)

[fit_options]
  iov_column = OCC
Option B — block IOV (correlated kappas):
block_kappa (KAPPA_CL, KAPPA_V) = [0.05, 0.01, 0.03]
Mixed Option A + B declarations are supported; a name may not appear in both.
The inner loop uses find_ebe_iov instead of find_ebe when kappas are present, jointly optimising BSV etas and all per-occasion kappas. The outer loop packs IOV omega after sigma in the packed vector. AD is not used for IOV gradients — finite differences are used instead.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Sampling Importance Resampling (SIR)
SIR is an optional post-estimation step that provides non-parametric parameter uncertainty estimates, more robust than the asymptotic covariance matrix for small datasets or boundary estimates.

Draw M parameter vectors from \(\mathcal{N}(\hat{\theta}, \hat{\Sigma})\)
Compute importance weights from OFV differences
Resample m vectors proportional to weights

The effective sample size (ESS) diagnostic indicates fit quality: ESS > m is excellent; ESS < 100 suggests the proposal is a poor match.
[fit_options]
  method        = focei
  covariance    = true
  sir           = true
  sir_samples   = 1000
  sir_resamples = 250
  sir_seed      = 42
Reference: Dosne et al. (2017), J Pharmacokinet Pharmacodyn 44(6):539-562.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Mu-referencing
When mu_referencing = true (default), ferx detects log-normal parameters from [individual_parameters] and re-centres ETA estimates on the current population mean each inner iteration. This improves conditioning for the SAEM M-step (closed-form EM update) and speeds inner-loop convergence for gradient-based methods. Disable with mu_referencing = false to reproduce pre-automatic-mu behaviour.

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Key fit_options reference








Key
Default
Description




method
focei
foce, focei, saem, gn, gn_hybrid, or a list e.g. [saem, focei]


maxiter
500
Max outer iterations


covariance
true
Compute covariance matrix and SEs


optimizer
slsqp
See optimizer table above


inner_maxiter
200
Max inner (EBE) iterations per subject


inner_tol
1e-4
Inner convergence gradient-norm tolerance


scale_params
true
Scale packed parameters to O(1) before optimizer


global_search
false
Run CRS2-LM pre-search before local optimizer


bloq_method
drop
drop or m3 (Beal M3 likelihood for BLOQ rows)


iov_column
—
Name of occasion column (required for IOV models)


optimizer_trace
false
Write per-iteration CSV to /tmp/ferx_trace_<pid>.csv

title: Estimation (FOCE/FOCEI)
text:
Relevant source files







File
Role




estimation/outer_optimizer.rs
optimize_population(), NLopt/BFGS/trust-region dispatch


estimation/inner_optimizer.rs
find_ebe(), gradient resolution (AD vs FD), EBE warm-start


estimation/saem.rs
SAEM algorithm, MH sampling, two-phase schedule


estimation/gauss_newton.rs
Gauss-Newton / BHHH with LM damping and line search


estimation/trust_region.rs
Newton trust-region with Steihaug CG subproblem


estimation/parameterization.rs
Pack/unpack, mu-referencing, log/Cholesky transforms


estimation/sir.rs
SIR importance resampling


stats/likelihood.rs

stats/residual_error.rs
Residual variance by error model, IWRES

title: ODE Solver
text: ferx-core includes an adaptive Runge-Kutta solver for models whose structural equations cannot be expressed analytically. It is invoked whenever a .ferx model file contains an [odes] block; analytical PK models bypass it entirely.

title: ODE Solver
text:
Algorithm: Dormand-Prince RK45
The solver implements the Dormand-Prince method (also called RK45 or DOPRI5) — the same family as Julia’s Tsit5(). It is an explicit, embedded Runge-Kutta pair:

5th-order solution used for advancing the state
4th-order embedded solution used only for error estimation
7 function evaluations per step (the last stage is reused as the first of the next step — FSAL property)

The Butcher tableau coefficients are hardcoded in ode/solver.rs from the original Dormand & Prince (1980) paper.
Default tolerances:



Parameter
Default




abstol
1e-6


reltol
1e-4


max_steps
10 000


initial_dt
0.1


min_dt
1e-12



These are appropriate for typical PK ODE systems (2–5 states, smooth dynamics, concentration units in μg/mL or mg/L). Tighter tolerances can be set via [fit_options] if needed.

title: ODE Solver
text:
Adaptive step control
At each candidate step the solver computes the scaled error norm:
err = ‖(y₅ − y₄) / (abstol + reltol · |y₅|)‖₂ / √n

If err ≤ 1: step accepted; next step size scaled up by min(5, 0.9 · err^(-0.2))
If err > 1: step rejected and retried at max(0.2, 0.9 · err^(-0.25)) · h

The solver errors if h falls below min_dt (signals a stiff or ill-posed system) or if the step count exceeds max_steps.

title: ODE Solver
ODE Solver
text:
Defining an ODE model
The [odes] section of a .ferx file specifies the right-hand side using a simple expression DSL. Derivatives are written as d/dt(STATE):
[odes]
  d/dt(depot)   = -KA * depot
  d/dt(central) = KA * depot - (CL / V) * central
Individual parameters from [individual_parameters] (CL, V, KA, …) are available as symbols. The observable compartment is matched to the CMT column in the data.

title: ODE Solver
section: When to use ODE vs analytical
text:
When to use ODE vs analytical







Situation
Recommendation




1-cpt or 2-cpt oral/IV, standard dosing
Use analytical (pk one_cpt_oral(...))


PD link model, transit compartments, enterohepatic circulation
Use [odes]


Saturable elimination (Michaelis-Menten), parallel pathways
Use [odes]



Analytical models are typically 10–50× faster per likelihood evaluation and support the Enzyme AD gradient path. ODE models currently fall back to finite-difference gradients.

title: ODE Solver
text:
Relevant source files







File
Role




ode/solver.rs
solve_ode(), Dormand-Prince coefficients, adaptive step loop


ode/predictions.rs
ode_predictions(), event handling, break-time construction


ode/mod.rs
Module re-exports

title: ferx
text:
open-source NLME · v0.1.2


Fe·Rx


A nonlinear mixed-effects engine for population PK/PD, written in Rust.

ferx fits models defined in a small, readable DSL — bracket-sectioned .ferx files — powered by a Rust backend with Enzyme automatic differentiation for exact gradients. FOCE/FOCEI estimation. MIT-licensed.


26 — iron, the element   2026 — launch year   MIT — license



R devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")


sh docker build -t ferx:latest .










[warfarin_pk.ferx] model

# one-compartment oral PK — warfarin

[parameters]
  theta TVCL(0.2, 0.001, 10.0)
  theta TVV(10.0, 0.1, 500.0)
  theta TVKA(1.5, 0.01, 50.0)
  omega ETA_CL ~ 0.09
  omega ETA_V  ~ 0.04
  omega ETA_KA ~ 0.30
  sigma PROP_ERR ~ 0.02

[individual_parameters]
  CL = TVCL * exp(ETA_CL)
  V  = TVV  * exp(ETA_V)
  KA = TVKA * exp(ETA_KA)

[structural_model]
  pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)

[error_model]
  DV ~ proportional(PROP_ERR)

Install
remotes::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")

FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1 R CMD INSTALL --no-lock .

title: Get started

Run the warfarin example
library(ferx)
paths <- ferx_example("warfarin")

title: Analytical PK Models
text: ferx-core provides closed-form analytical solutions for one-, two-, and three-compartment PK models. These are the preferred structural model type: they are faster than ODE integration and support the Enzyme automatic differentiation path for gradient computation.

title: Analytical PK Models
section: Invoking a model in the DSL
text:
Invoking a model in the DSL
The [structural_model] section of a .ferx file selects an analytical model with the pk keyword:
[structural_model]
  pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)
Named arguments map individual-parameter symbols to the model’s PK slots. The available models and their required arguments are listed below.

title: Analytical PK Models
text:
One-compartment models
All one-compartment solutions are in pk/one_compartment.rs.

IV bolus
pk one_cpt_iv_bolus(cl=CL, v=V)
\[C(t) = \frac{\text{Dose}}{V} \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}, \quad k = \frac{CL}{V}\]


IV infusion
pk one_cpt_iv_infusion(cl=CL, v=V)
During infusion (\(t \le T_{\text{inf}}\)): \[C(t) = \frac{\text{Rate}}{CL} \cdot (1 - e^{-k t})\]
After infusion (\(t > T_{\text{inf}}\)): \[C(t) = \frac{\text{Rate}}{CL} \cdot (1 - e^{-k T_{\text{inf}}}) \cdot e^{-k(t - T_{\text{inf}})}\]


Oral absorption
pk one_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA)
pk one_cpt_oral_f(cl=CL, v=V, ka=KA, f=F)   # with bioavailability
\[C(t) = \frac{F \cdot \text{Dose} \cdot K_A}{V (K_A - k)} \left(e^{-k t} - e^{-K_A t}\right)\]
When \(K_A \approx k\) (flip-flop, within 1e-6), the L’Hôpital limit is used to avoid catastrophic cancellation:
\[C(t) = \frac{F \cdot \text{Dose} \cdot K_A}{V} \cdot t \cdot e^{-k t}\]

title: Analytical PK Models
text:
Two-compartment models
All two-compartment solutions are in pk/two_compartment.rs.
Macro rate constants are derived from micro-constants using Vieta’s formula to avoid subtracting two nearly-equal large numbers when \(s \gg \sqrt{s^2 - 4d}\):
let alpha = (s + disc) / 2.0;
let beta  = d / alpha;           // Vieta: α·β = d
where \(s = k_{10} + k_{12} + k_{21}\) and \(d = k_{10} \cdot k_{21}\).

IV bolus
pk two_cpt_iv_bolus(cl=CL, v1=V1, q=Q, v2=V2)
\[C(t) = A e^{-\alpha t} + B e^{-\beta t}\]
\[A = \frac{\text{Dose}}{V_1} \cdot \frac{\alpha - k_{21}}{\alpha - \beta}, \quad B = \frac{\text{Dose}}{V_1} \cdot \frac{k_{21} - \beta}{\alpha - \beta}\]


IV infusion
pk two_cpt_iv_infusion(cl=CL, v1=V1, q=Q, v2=V2)
Superposition of the bolus solution across the infusion duration; switches to bolus formula when duration ≤ 0.


Oral absorption
pk two_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v1=V1, q=Q, v2=V2, ka=KA)
pk two_cpt_oral_f(cl=CL, v1=V1, q=Q, v2=V2, ka=KA, f=F)
\[C(t) = Ae^{-\alpha t} + Be^{-\beta t} + Ce^{-K_A t}\]
Coefficients derived analytically; degenerate cases (\(K_A \approx \alpha\) or \(K_A \approx \beta\)) handled via L’Hôpital limits.

title: Analytical PK Models
text:
Three-compartment models
Three-compartment solutions are in pk/three_compartment.rs. Macro rate constants (\(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\gamma\)) are solved from the characteristic polynomial using Cardano’s formula.
pk three_cpt_iv_bolus(cl=CL, v1=V1, q2=Q2, v2=V2, q3=Q3, v3=V3)
pk three_cpt_iv_infusion(cl=CL, v1=V1, q2=Q2, v2=V2, q3=Q3, v3=V3)
pk three_cpt_oral(cl=CL, v1=V1, q2=Q2, v2=V2, q3=Q3, v3=V3, ka=KA)

title: Analytical PK Models
Analytical PK Models
text:
PkParams layout
PK parameters are passed through a fixed-layout [f64; 8] array rather than a HashMap so that Enzyme’s reverse-mode AD can differentiate through them:








Index
Symbol
Description




0
CL
Clearance


1
V / V1
Volume (central)


2
Q
Intercompartmental clearance (central ↔︎ peripheral 1)


3
V2
Peripheral volume 1


4
KA
Oral absorption rate constant


5
F
Bioavailability (default 1.0)


6
Q3
Intercompartmental clearance (central ↔︎ peripheral 2; 3-cmt)


7
V3
Peripheral volume 2 (3-cmt)



Unused slots default to 0.0 (except F, which defaults to 1.0).

title: Analytical PK Models
text:
Relevant source files







File
Role




pk/one_compartment.rs
1-cpt IV bolus, infusion, oral, oral-with-F


pk/two_compartment.rs
2-cpt IV bolus, infusion, oral, oral-with-F; macro rate constants


pk/three_compartment.rs
3-cpt IV bolus and infusion; Cardano characteristic roots


pk/mod.rs
compute_predictions(), multi-dose superposition, TV-covariate dispatch


types.rs
PkParams layout constants (PK_IDX_CL, PK_IDX_V, …)

title: Data Format
text: ferx-core reads data in NONMEM-compatible CSV format. This is the standard format shared by NONMEM, Monolix, and most population PK tools.

title: Data Format
text:
Required columns



Column
Type
Description




ID
string/numeric
Subject identifier


TIME
numeric
Time relative to first dose


DV
numeric
Dependent variable (observed concentration)

title: Data Format
text:
Optional standard columns









Column
Type
Default
Description




EVID
integer
0
Event ID — see table below


AMT
numeric
0
Dose amount (dose rows only)


CMT
integer
1
Compartment number (1-indexed)


RATE
numeric
0
Infusion rate; 0 = bolus


MDV
integer
0
Missing DV flag; 1 = skip this observation


II
numeric
0
Interdose interval for steady-state records


SS
integer
0
Steady-state flag; 1 = assume steady state before TIME=0


CENS
integer
0
BLOQ flag; 1 = DV is below LLOQ (DV carries the LLOQ value)



Missing values may be represented as . or as an empty cell.
Column names are case-insensitive: ID, Id, and id are all recognised.

title: Data Format
text:
Event types (EVID)







EVID
Meaning




0
Observation record. DV is used for estimation.


1
Dose record. AMT is administered to compartment CMT.


2
Covariate-change marker. Compartment amounts are unchanged but the PK parameter expressions are re-evaluated using this row’s covariate values. No-op when all covariates are time-constant.


4
Reset + dose. All compartment amounts are zeroed before dosing.

title: Data Format
text:
Covariate columns
Any column not in the standard set above is treated as a covariate.

Time-constant covariates: the first non-missing value per subject is used.
Time-varying covariates: Last Observation Carried Forward (LOCF) per row — individual-parameter expressions are re-evaluated at each dose and observation row using that row’s covariate snapshot.

Covariate names are matched case-insensitively against names in [individual_parameters] expressions. Numeric covariate columns that are not the IOV occasion column are candidates for ferx_eta_cov() in the R layer.

Time-varying covariate support
Time-varying covariates are supported for all analytical models (1/2/3-cpt) and all ODE-defined models. The Enzyme gradient fast path is also event-driven for TV-covariate subjects on analytical models — the AD path does not fall back to finite differences when covariates vary.

title: Data Format
text:
Occasion column (IOV)
When using Inter-Occasion Variability, add an integer occasion-index column and name it with iov_column in [fit_options]:
[fit_options]
  iov_column = OCC
The OCC column:

Contains positive integer occasion indices (e.g. 1, 2, 3…) — one per row
Applies to both dose and observation rows
Is excluded from covariate auto-detection
Does not need to start at 1 or be consecutive; different numbers mean different occasions with independent kappa EBEs

title: Data Format
text:
BLOQ observations
Set CENS = 1 on any row where the measured concentration is below the lower limit of quantification. The DV column must contain the LLOQ value (not a zero or missing). Pair with bloq_method = m3 in [fit_options] to enable Beal M3 likelihood-based BLOQ handling; the default drop simply discards these rows.

title: Data Format
text:
Infusion doses
Set RATE > 0 on a dose row to specify an infusion. Duration is derived as AMT / RATE:
ID,TIME,DV,EVID,AMT,CMT,RATE,MDV
1,0,.,1,500,1,50,1
This administers 500 units over 10 hours at a rate of 50 units/hour.
Dose and infusion routing on analytical models:








CMT
IV models
Oral models




1
Central compartment
Depot — bolus is absorbed via first-order KA


2
Peripheral 1 infusion (2-cpt and 3-cpt)
Central compartment infusion


3
Peripheral 2 infusion (3-cpt only)
— (panics with a clear message)

title: Data Format
text:
Steady-state dosing
Set SS = 1 and II to the dosing interval on a dose row to instruct ferx-core to compute the compartment amounts that would result from an infinite history of doses spaced II apart before TIME = 0:
ID,TIME,DV,EVID,AMT,CMT,SS,II,MDV
1,0,.,1,100,1,1,12,1
1,0.5,25.3,0,.,.,.,.,0

title: Data Format
text:
Multiple doses
Each dose is a separate row with EVID = 1. Analytical models sum contributions via multi-dose superposition:
ID,TIME,DV,EVID,AMT,CMT,MDV
1,0,.,1,100,1,1
1,0.5,9.49,0,.,.,0
1,12,.,1,100,1,1
1,12.5,15.2,0,.,.,0
1,24,.,1,100,1,1
1,24.5,18.1,0,.,.,0

title: Data Format
text:
Full example with covariates
ID,TIME,DV,EVID,AMT,CMT,RATE,MDV,WT,CRCL
1,0,.,1,100,1,0,1,70,95
1,0.5,9.49,0,.,.,.,0,70,95
1,1,14.42,0,.,.,.,0,70,95
1,2,17.56,0,.,.,.,0,70,95
1,4,15.23,0,.,.,.,0,70,95
1,8,10.15,0,.,.,.,0,70,95
2,0,.,1,150,1,0,1,85,110
2,0.5,14.2,0,.,.,.,0,85,110
2,1,21.3,0,.,.,.,0,85,110

Dose records (EVID=1): MDV=1, DV=.
Observation records (EVID=0): MDV=0, valid DV
Covariates (WT, CRCL) are extra columns; matched to [individual_parameters] by name

title: Data Format
text:
Relevant source files







File
Role




io/datareader.rs
read_nonmem_csv() — full CSV parser, LOCF, covariate detection


types.rs
Subject, DoseEvent, Population — parsed representation

title: Autodiff (Enzyme)
text: ferx-core uses automatic differentiation (AD) to compute exact gradients of the FOCEI individual NLL with respect to the ETA vector. Exact gradients make the inner L-BFGS loop faster and more reliable than finite differences, especially as the number of random effects grows.

title: Autodiff (Enzyme)
text:
Why exact gradients matter
The FOCEI inner loop calls \(\nabla_\eta \text{NLL}(\eta)\) at every L-BFGS step. For a model with \(n_\eta\) random effects, central finite differences require \(2n_\eta\) model evaluations per gradient call. Reverse-mode AD computes the full gradient in ~1–3 forward passes regardless of \(n_\eta\).
Empirically (measured with FERX_TIME_GRADIENTS=1 on 1-cpt oral and 2-cpt infusion models): reverse-mode AD is 1.5–5× faster per L-BFGS gradient call than central FD across the tested range of models, even accounting for the tape/backward overhead.

title: Autodiff (Enzyme)
text:
Enzyme
ferx uses Enzyme — a compiler plugin that differentiates LLVM IR directly. It is not a symbolic or trace-based AD library; it transforms the compiled bitcode of the forward pass into its reverse (adjoint) at compile time.
Build requirements:

channel = "enzyme" in rust-toolchain.toml (a custom Rust fork)
-Z autodiff=Enable in .cargo/config.toml (ferx-r’s build script writes this automatically)
Only required at compile time — the resulting binary has the AD tape baked in and runs on any system

CI / no-Enzyme builds: set FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1 before building. ferx falls back to central finite differences. All fits still work; wall time increases ~2–5× on large \(n_\eta\) models.

title: Autodiff (Enzyme)
Autodiff (Enzyme)
Autodiff (Enzyme)
text:
Building without Enzyme
# Install without Enzyme (for development, CI, Windows)
FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1 R CMD INSTALL --no-lock .

# Or for the Rust crate directly
cargo build --no-default-features --features ci
With FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1, the autodiff feature flag is disabled, the Enzyme-gated #[cfg(feature = "autodiff")] blocks compile out, and resolve_gradient_method always returns Fd. The gradient_used field in FitResult reports "finite_differences".

title: Autodiff (Enzyme)
text:
Relevant source files







File
Role




ad/dual.rs
Dual<f64> forward-mode number type and arithmetic impls


ad/ad_gradients.rs
Enzyme reverse-mode gradient entry points (#[autodiff])


ad/event_driven_ad.rs
AD-compatible event-driven propagator for TV-covariate subjects


ad/event_driven_ad_jac.rs
Jacobian accumulation for Enzyme reverse mode


estimation/inner_optimizer.rs
resolve_gradient_method(), gradient dispatch, timing counters

title: Architecture
text:
.ferx model file  ──► parser/model_parser.rs  ──► CompiledModel
NONMEM CSV        ──► io/datareader.rs         ──► Population
                                                        │
                                               api::fit()
                                                        │
                              ┌─────────────────────────┤
                              │                         │
                    outer_optimizer            inner_optimizer
                    (population θ, Ω, σ)     (per-subject EBE η)
                              │                         │
                         pk/  or  ode/           stats/likelihood
                    (predictions)              (NLL evaluation)
                              │
                         FitResult  ──► R via extendr FFI
The outer optimizer iterates over population parameters (theta, omega, sigma). For each candidate parameter set it calls the inner optimizer once per subject to find the empirical Bayes estimate (EBE) of that subject’s random effects. Predictions are routed to the analytical PK models in pk/ or the RK45 ODE solver in ode/, depending on whether the model file has an [odes] block.

title: Architecture
text:
.ferx model file  ──► parser/model_parser.rs  ──► CompiledModel
NONMEM CSV        ──► io/datareader.rs         ──► Population
                                                        │
                                               api::fit()
                                                        │
                              ┌─────────────────────────┤
                              │                         │
                    outer_optimizer            inner_optimizer
                    (population θ, Ω, σ)     (per-subject EBE η)
                              │                         │
                         pk/  or  ode/           stats/likelihood
                    (predictions)              (NLL evaluation)
                              │
                         FitResult  ──► R via extendr FFI
The outer optimizer iterates over population parameters (theta, omega, sigma). For each candidate parameter set it calls the inner optimizer once per subject to find the empirical Bayes estimate (EBE) of that subject’s random effects. Predictions are routed to the analytical PK models in pk/ or the RK45 ODE solver in ode/, depending on whether the model file has an [odes] block.

title: Architecture
text:
FFI boundary
ferx-r wraps ferx-core via extendr. The glue layer in src/rust/src/lib.rs (ferx-r repo) exposes three #[extendr] functions:

ferx_rust_fit — calls api::run_model_with_data, returns a named R list
ferx_rust_simulate — calls api::simulate, returns an R data frame
ferx_rust_predict — calls api::predict, returns an R data frame

Conversion helpers:

fit_result_to_list() — unpacks FitResult into an R list (theta, omega, sigma, SEs, OFV/AIC/BIC, diagnostics, model structure, warnings)
sdtab_to_dataframe() — converts Vec<SdtabRow> into a data frame with columns PRED, IPRED, CWRES, IWRES, and one column per ETA

title: Architecture
text:
Key types

Subject
Holds all per-subject data after the NONMEM CSV is parsed.
pub struct Subject {
    pub id: String,
    pub doses: Vec<DoseEvent>,
    pub obs_times: Vec<f64>,
    pub observations: Vec<f64>,
    pub obs_cmts: Vec<usize>,
    pub covariates: HashMap<String, f64>,           // static (first non-missing)
    pub dose_covariates: Vec<HashMap<String, f64>>, // LOCF snapshot per dose event
    pub obs_covariates: Vec<HashMap<String, f64>>,  // LOCF snapshot per observation
    pub cens: Vec<u8>,       // 0 = observed, 1 = BLOQ (LLOQ stored in observations)
    pub occasions: Vec<u32>, // IOV occasion index per observation; empty if no IOV
    // ...
}
When a subject has no time-varying covariates, dose_covariates and obs_covariates are empty and callers fall through to the static covariates map. Methods obs_cov(j) / dose_cov(k) encapsulate this fallback.


DoseEvent
pub struct DoseEvent {
    pub time: f64,
    pub amt: f64,
    pub cmt: usize,
    pub rate: f64,
    pub duration: f64,  // derived: amt / rate when rate > 0, else 0
    pub ss: bool,
    pub ii: f64,
}


PkParams
Fixed-size array instead of HashMap<String, f64> — required for Enzyme AD.
pub struct PkParams {
    pub values: [f64; 8],  // MAX_PK_PARAMS = 8
}
// Index:  0=CL  1=V  2=Q  3=V2  4=KA  5=F  6=Q3  7=V3
F defaults to 1.0. Unused slots default to 0.0.


ModelParameters
The full set of population parameters under estimation.
pub struct ModelParameters {
    pub theta: Vec<f64>,
    pub theta_names: Vec<String>,
    pub theta_lower: Vec<f64>,
    pub theta_upper: Vec<f64>,
    pub theta_fixed: Vec<bool>,
    pub omega: OmegaMatrix,  // caches Cholesky factor + free_mask
    pub sigma: SigmaVector,
    // ...
}
OmegaMatrix caches the Cholesky factor alongside the full matrix and carries a free_mask (DMatrix<bool>) that records which off-diagonal entries are free parameters versus structural zeros from block_omega declarations.

title: Architecture
text:
Internal parameterisation
The outer optimizer works on an unconstrained packed vector x:
x = [log(theta_free), chol(Omega_BSV), log(sigma), chol(Omega_IOV)]

Theta: log-transformed to enforce positivity; unpacked with exp()
Omega: lower Cholesky factor; diagonal elements log-transformed; unpacked with L * L'
Sigma: log-transformed, same as theta

This ensures the optimizer never visits invalid parameter regions (negative variances, non-positive-definite omega). Parameter scaling (scale_params = true) additionally normalizes the packed vector to O(1) before each optimizer call to improve conditioning when parameters span several orders of magnitude.

title: Architecture
text:
Parallelism
The outer optimizer evaluates subjects in parallel via rayon:
let subject_nlls: Vec<f64> = population
    .subjects
    .par_iter()
    .map(|subject| foce_subject_nll(subject, &params, &model))
    .collect();
The inner optimizer (EBE search) is per-subject and single-threaded; no nested parallelism is used. Thread count is controlled by FitOptions::n_threads, forwarded to rayon’s global thread pool at the start of each fit call.

title: Architecture
text:
Source tree
src/
├── api.rs                     public entry points
├── types.rs                   Subject, DoseEvent, PkParams, ModelParameters, FitResult
├── lib.rs                     crate root
├── build_info.rs              compile-time build metadata
├── cancel.rs                  cooperative cancellation token
├── parser/
│   └── model_parser.rs        .ferx DSL → CompiledModel
├── io/
│   ├── datareader.rs          read_nonmem_csv → Population
│   └── output.rs              sdtab / YAML writer
├── estimation/
│   ├── outer_optimizer.rs     population-level loop, NLopt/BFGS dispatch
│   ├── inner_optimizer.rs     per-subject EBE, gradient resolution (AD/FD)
│   ├── saem.rs                SAEM with MH sampling, two-phase schedule
│   ├── gauss_newton.rs        Gauss-Newton / BHHH with LM damping
│   ├── trust_region.rs        Newton trust-region (Steihaug CG subproblem)
│   ├── parameterization.rs    mu-referencing, log/logit transforms, pack/unpack
│   ├── sir.rs                 SIR importance resampling
│   └── trace.rs               iteration trace CSV writer
├── pk/
│   ├── one_compartment.rs     1-cpt IV bolus/infusion/oral analytical solutions
│   ├── two_compartment.rs     2-cpt analytical solutions, Vieta macro-rate constants
│   ├── three_compartment.rs   3-cpt IV bolus/infusion, Cardano characteristic roots
│   └── event_driven.rs        multi-dose superposition, TV-covariate dispatch
├── ode/
│   ├── solver.rs              RK45 Dormand-Prince adaptive integrator
│   └── predictions.rs         event handling, break-time construction
├── stats/
│   ├── likelihood.rs          FOCE/FOCEI NLL, CWRES, population objective
│   ├── residual_error.rs      IWRES, residual variance by error model type
│   └── special.rs             log-gamma, logit, log-normal CDF
├── ad/
│   ├── dual.rs                Dual<f64> forward-mode number type
│   ├── ad_gradients.rs        Enzyme reverse-mode gradient entry points
│   ├── event_driven_ad.rs     AD-compatible event-driven propagator
│   └── event_driven_ad_jac.rs Jacobian accumulation for Enzyme reverse mode
└── bin/
    ├── run_model.rs           CLI entry point
    └── generate_data.rs       synthetic data generator
